doom.driver: Iran's IRGC says it attacked sites linked to the US military in Kuwait, Bahrain and Oman after latest US bombardment.
doom.blurb: THE WAR IS NOW REGIONAL, ACTUALLY
doom.summary: Iran has stopped retaliating against the US and started hitting everyone else — Kuwait, Bahrain, Oman — which is the geopolitical equivalent of losing a bar fight and punching the bystanders. Three weekends of consecutive strikes have a rhythm now, and rhythms have a way of becoming permanent features. Meanwhile Lindsey Graham's death shrinks the Senate GOP margin to a razor's edge right as the Iran war, FISA, and government funding all land on the calendar simultaneously.

factors.name: Geopolitics
factors.detail: US-Iran exchanges now in their third consecutive weekend, with Iran expanding targets to US allies across the Gulf — a meaningful regional escalation beyond the bilateral pattern.

factors.name: Governance
factors.detail: Graham's death leaves Senate Republicans with a razor-thin margin heading into a brutal legislative sprint covering war powers, FISA, and the budget — dysfunction risk is real.

factors.name: AI Capability
factors.detail: GPT-5.6 adoption accelerating in production (2.2x faster, 27% cheaper per Hacker News), compounding last week's mathematical proof milestone into a normalizing capability curve.

factors.name: Climate
factors.detail: Wildfires burning near Paris for the first time requiring southern-France-style air tankers, plus French nuclear plants stressed by warm rivers — Western Europe's infrastructure is visibly straining.

factors.name: Wild Card
factors.detail: UK banning Iran's Revolutionary Guard and citing Iranian-backed attacks on Jewish sites adds another pressure node to an already overloaded diplomatic circuit board.

doom.dalle_prompt: Pixel art, 16-bit SNES style, no text, no antialiasing — silhouette composition, dark crimson and charcoal palette, camera positioned low at ground level inside a sparse Gulf Arab apartment, shooting toward a tall narrow window where two silhouetted figures stand motionless side by side, a parent and a teenage child, their outlines stark black against the window's deep crimson glow bleeding in from outside, the parent's arm wrapped around the child's shoulder, both utterly still, the room behind them rendered in near-total darkness with only faint charcoal outlines of a low wooden table, a small television set casting no light, a prayer rug rolled against the wall, the window frame itself cracked at one corner, thin curtain panels pushed aside, the exterior glow outside the glass suggesting distant fire reflected off low desert haze, the crimson light painting the edges of the two figures in thin burning lines while their faces remain completely obscured in shadow, the floor tiles a faded ochre pattern barely visible in the darkness, a single mobile phone face-down on the table behind them its screen dark, the composition entirely still and suffocating, all drama concentrated in the silhouettes against that burning window glow.

dawn.driver: Four children with terminal brain cancer saved by new cell therapy. An experimental immunotherapy has beaten aggressive brain tumours in a handful of children, and a personalised version is now being tested on more patients
dawn.blurb: CHILDREN WITH TERMINAL CANCER ARE SURVIVING
dawn.summary: Against a backdrop of escalating conflict and geopolitical dread, medicine quietly delivered something extraordinary: children diagnosed with terminal brain cancer — some of the most aggressive, least survivable tumors known — walked away. An experimental immunotherapy didn't just slow progression; it beat the tumors outright in multiple young patients. The researchers aren't stopping there — a personalized version is now entering broader trials, meaning this breakthrough may scale. On a day when headlines are soaked in fire and strikes, scientists were saving kids.

factors.name: Science
factors.detail: A cell therapy achieving remission in terminal pediatric brain cancer is one of the most significant oncology signals in years — these tumors carry near-zero survival rates under standard care.

factors.name: Cooperation
factors.detail: The transition to a personalized trial reflects deep collaboration between immunologists, oncologists, and clinical teams working across institutions toward a shared goal.

factors.name: Human Spirit
factors.detail: Researchers continuing to chase cures for dying children — the hardest, most emotionally costly work in medicine — is a quiet testament to what humans do when the stakes are highest.

factors.name: Environment
factors.detail: Colorado's new EV battery recycling law offers a pragmatic legislative model for closing the economics gap that has stalled green infrastructure scaling.

factors.name: Wild Card
factors.detail: New Scientist's report that a mental speed-training game may help men's brains clear Alzheimer's-linked amyloid adds another thread to the growing tapestry of dementia prevention strategies.

dawn.dalle_prompt: Pixel art, 16-bit SNES style, no text, no antialiasing. Night scene, all light from artificial sources, deep shadows dominating. A child's bedroom at night, seen from a low angle beside the bed, deep teal-black darkness filling the corners and ceiling, a single warm amber bedside lamp casting a tight golden pool across rumpled superhero-themed blankets. Within that pool of light, a tablet screen glows with vivid emerald and gold, displaying a news article whose lead image shows a smiling child in a hospital chair — the screen is the only source of saturated color in the room, rich gold headline text catching the eye without being legible. A small hand reaches from beneath the blankets toward the screen, tiny fingers mid-scroll, alive with curiosity. On the nightstand beside the lamp sits a half-drunk glass of water and a child's crayon drawing of a stick figure with a superhero cape, pinned under a corner of the tablet case. The lamp's amber warmth bleeds into the tablet's emerald glow, the two light sources merging on the blanket surface in a pool of gold and green. Deep shadows eat the walls. The room feels quiet, late, private — a child somewhere in the world, reading this story in the dark, alive.
